title: Saving the Civil Service Act
plain_summary: This bill stops the government from creating new job types that would make it easier to fire federal workers. It says government jobs can only be moved to job categories that existed before September 2020, and workers must agree before their jobs can be changed. The bill also limits how many employees can be moved to different job types and requires special approval from the government's hiring office. This protects federal workers from losing important job protections that make it harder to fire them unfairly.
last_action_text: ASSUMING FIRST SPONSORSHIP - Mr. Walkinshaw asked unanimous consent that he may hereafter be considered as the first sponsor of H.R. 492, a bill originally introduced by Representative Connolly, for t
subjects: Government employee pay, benefits, personnel management

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Saving the Civil Service Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Limitations on Excepting Positions from Competitive Service and Transferring Positions
sections.text: This section sets rules for how government jobs can be moved between different types of employment systems. The competitive service means jobs that people get through fair competition like tests or applications that anyone can apply for. The excepted service means jobs that do not require this open competition process. A government job in the competitive service can only be moved out of that system if it goes into one of the existing job categories called schedules A through E that were in place on September thirtieth two thousand twenty. These moves must follow the same rules that were in effect on that date. Jobs already in the excepted service can only be moved to those same approved schedules. Any government agency that wants to move an occupied job to schedule C of the excepted service must first get permission from the Director of the Office of Personnel Management. During any four year presidential term, an agency cannot move more than one percent of its total workforce from competitive service to excepted service, or five employees, whichever number is larger. This limit is based on how many employees the agency had on the first day of that presidential term. No employee can be moved from one type of job schedule to another without giving their written permission first. This includes moves within the excepted service and moves from competitive service to excepted service. These rules also apply to jobs at the Department of Veterans Affairs under chapters seventy three and seventy four, even though those jobs normally have different rules. The Director of the Office of Personnel Management must create regulations to make these rules work. In this section, agency means any department, agency, or part of the Federal Government. Competitive service means jobs defined in section twenty one oh two of title five of United States Code. Director means the Director of the Office of Personnel Management. Excepted service means jobs defined in section twenty one oh three of title five of United States Code.
